story:
Twilight bled into the sky, the moon a blood-red coin staining the dusk—an omen the hunter took as routine. Scarred and lean, a peculiar saw-like blade strapped to his back, he climbed toward the mountaintop bastion. Once it belonged to the count of the vale; now a coven of night-creatures had taken it for their refuge. Peasants from a nearby village—afraid and desperate—had begged him to clear the place, offering a sagging purse of gold, a sickly horse, a bite of food, and even the company of a local woman for the road. He knew such barter was ugly and refused to dwell on it. Work was work; the night would not wait.

By the time he reached the walls, the land of Drazcroll lay under full darkness. At the barred gate he called out with a cocky cheerfulness that sounded like a dare. Wolves howled. A shuffling shape lumbered from the forecourt—more were-dog than wolf, ears drooping, posture unsure. It barked a threat that didn’t quite fit its frame. The hunter, unruffled, asked for the one who ruled the bastion now. He had studied vampires since he was a child, he said; he knew they were cunning, not stupid. He wished to make an arrangement.

The creature grumbled, but went. The portcullis rose with a sudden clatter—as if pulled by an invisible hand. Magic, then. The hunter stepped through, just ahead of the thundering fall of iron behind him, and crossed a forecourt alive with whispering voices that felt less like spirits than a house’s way of scaring off the ignorant. A disembodied voice bade him come. He followed it through halls that were eerie yet oddly homely, warmth fighting with the chill that threaded the stone.

In a grand solar, fire ripe on the hearth, sat a powdered gentleman in foppish finery, eyes bright, lips dark as spilled wine. He reclined with a heavy old tome, then rose in a flourish when the hunter appeared. The blade was set against the wall; talk first, always.

The creature’s welcome was theatrical, almost kindly. He offered a drink the hunter would not want. The hunter called him “kid,” and the vampire’s smile twitched. Young, the hunter judged: a fledgling who didn’t love what he’d become, but loved the power it seemed to promise. Why else cast out the count, menace the hamlets, and put on the costume of a lord?

They traded barbs like fencers. The vampire’s voice sang and spun; his tailcoat swirled as he poured himself a chalice of something thick and red. He sniffed and frowned in delighted curiosity. Human blood clung to the hunter like a ghost—old, frightened. Did the man kill humans as well as monsters? Did he eat them? He thought.

"Only when I have to," the hunter said, "No, I don't eat them." He did not drink blood; it soured the belly. Flesh, in the bleakest moments, had kept him alive. He spoke without pride and without apology. The vampire tittered, intrigued—how queer, how fascinating, a mirror turned the wrong way: a human whose hunger in extremity echoed a vampire’s need.

“Well then,” the hunter said at last, stepping close enough to see the fire shine on powdered cheek and sharpened tooth, “let’s speak plainly.”

He had come to make a deal.
